problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem A (continuous/dynamics): design a model for the optimal power-output distribution of a road cyclist over a time-trial course. Sub-tasks: (1) define power-curve "profiles" for two rider types and both genders (one a time-trial specialist); (2) apply the model to ≥3 specific time-trial courses; (3) analyze weather (wind) sensitivity; (4) analyze sensitivity to the rider deviating from the prescribed power plan; (5) extend to a 6-person team time trial. Paper #2209466 frames it as a minimum-finish-time optimal-control problem: choose power as a function of track position, subject to a physiological energy budget and course/aero constraints.
models_used: 3-Tank / three-component bioenergetic model (Morton 1986) as the physiological backbone, specialized into a two-pool 'Aerobic + Anaerobic Energy Fatigue Model'
Critical-power / two-parameter power-duration model (Monod-Scherrer hyperbolic P=W'/t) plus Morton 3-parameter critical-power extension as literature grounding for the power curve
Piecewise analytic power-time (power) curve: P(t)=a+W_N/t for P>=FTP (anaerobic, hyperbolic) and P(t)=-b·ln t + c for P<FTP (aerobic, logarithmic), pinned by the constraint P(3600s)=FTP
Aerobic-pool consumption rate v_o(P_o)=gamma·delta^{P_o}; anaerobic consumption rate v_N=alpha·P_N; anaerobic recovery rate v_R=alpha·(FTP-P_o)/beta (recovery factor beta ~2x)
Ordinary Least Squares curve fitting of the power curve to real Strava power-duration data, separately for men and women

Riding Resistance Model: R_T = 0.5·rho·A_p·C_D·v_f^2 + C_R·M·g, with empirical sub-models for air density rho(altitude/temp via barometric formula), projected frontal area A_p(height,mass), drag coefficient C_D=4.45·m^-0.45, and C_R=0.003
Newtonian point-mass longitudinal dynamics on a slope: F - Mg·sin(theta) - R_T = M·a; cornering treated as circular motion giving a speed-cap checkpoint at sharp turns
Abstract Course Model: altitude as f(x), slope theta=phi(x)=f'(x), corners as a binary indicator g(x) (1 at sharp turn, 0 elsewhere)
Single-objective programming: minimize finish time; decision variable = power at each position; constraints = aerobic & anaerobic energy >= 0 and corner speed limits
Dynamic Programming solver over discretized states (remaining aerobic energy, remaining anaerobic energy, end speed) per track segment — the core optimization engine
Wind-corrected resistance model: R_T = 0.5·rho·A_p·C_D·(v_f + v_w·cos(delta))^2·sign(...) + C_R·M·g, swept over 8 directions x 3 wind speeds (24 scenarios) for sensitivity
Monte-Carlo deviation analysis: MATLAB random perturbation of the optimal power plan, mean-absolute-deviation vs finish-time degradation

Team Time Trial extension: drafting drag-reduction coefficient C_dr per queue position (6-rider table, ~95%/68%/67%... ) with new discrete decision variable h(x) for Draft timing and a Drop strategy, solved by more complex DP
key_techniques: Decoupling the problem into two clean layers: a physiology layer (power curve / energy budget) and a mechanics layer (resistance + course), joined only through the DP — keeps each model interpretable

Casting an inherently continuous optimal-control problem as a discrete dynamic program by discretizing the state space (aerobic energy, anaerobic energy, terminal speed) and exhaustively trying all powers at each segment

Encoding the rider taxonomy (Sprinter, Puncheur, Rouleur, Time-Trial Specialist) directly as named model parameters (FTP, beta, W_N, weight, b) and visualizing them on a radar chart — turns a qualitative classification into quantitative parameter sets
Grounding empirical constants in cited sports-science / aerodynamics literature rather than inventing them (A_p, C_D, C_R, rho formulas all referenced)
Reducing cornering to a 'speed checkpoint' constraint instead of full 2D dynamics — a deliberate dimensional reduction that keeps the DP 1D along arc length

Two complementary sensitivity analyses: structured grid sweep for wind (24 cases) and stochastic perturbation for execution error, each with a quantified threshold (16% time change for wind; ~10% time change at ~5% power deviation)
Translating the abstract optimum into a coach-usable artifact: per-checkpoint acceptable arrival-time windows (Max/Min time table) that guarantee finishing within 1 minute of optimal
Building the team model as a minimal modification (one drag coefficient + one new decision variable) of the individual model rather than a from-scratch model — demonstrates generalizability
why_it_won: "From a judging standpoint the paper hits the rubric squarely. It directly answers every sub-question (rider profiles by type AND gender, three real courses incl. Tokyo Olympic and UCI Flanders, wind sensitivity, deviation sensitivity, team extension) — completeness is the first thing judges check. Its central modeling choice is correct and non-trivial: it recognizes the task as a constrained minimum-time optimal-control problem and solves it with dynamic programming over a physically meaningful state (two energy reserves + speed), which is far stronger than the naive 'hold constant power' or pure-physics approaches many teams submit. The physiology is credibly anchored in the real critical-power / 3-tank literature and validated by OLS fitting against actual Strava data, so the power curve is not hand-waved. It produces concrete, defensible numbers (finish times per rider/course, e.g. UCI specialist 3499s) and interprets them with sport-logical conclusions (sprinters attack early, specialists steadier, hillier courses force more power fluctuation). Finally it delivers the required real-world deliverable — a one-page Directeur/rider guidance with a P-D plan and tolerance windows — which signals practical usefulness. Good structure, real data, a correct optimization core, quantified sensitivity, and an actionable handoff are exactly what move a paper from Meritorious to Outstanding."
section_highlights: "Strong, scannable IMRaD-plus structure: a dense one-page summary that states all five results up front; an 'Our Work' narrative paired with a full mermaid-style flowchart (Fig 2) that maps every sub-model to the DP; explicit Assumptions-with-justifications and a Notations table before any math. The physiology section builds the power curve from first principles (aerobic vs anaerobic pools) then validates it against real data with men/women fits. The optimization section cleanly separates Resistance Model, Abstract Course Model, and the DP solver, each with force diagrams. Results are interpreted, not just plotted — each figure is followed by sport-meaningful conclusions and a cross-rider/cross-course comparison bar chart of finish times. Sensitivity gets its own section with a 24-case wind radar and a deviation table with explicit percentage thresholds. The paper ends with a standout judge-pleasing artifact: a self-contained 'Men Elite Individual Time Trial Guidance' memo (track + weather + rider analysis + P-D plan + checkpoint time table) that reads like a real race brief. Strengths/Weaknesses are stated honestly."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The piecewise power curve P(t)=a+W_N/t vs -b·ln t + c has loosely-defined coefficients (a,b,c described as 'adjustment coefficients' pinned only by P(3600)=FTP) and the notation is sloppy in places — several symbols (e.g. in eqs 3,4,6,12) are referenced in prose but never cleanly defined, which is a fit-and-finish weakness

The aerobic consumption rate v_o = gamma·delta^{P_o} (an exponential in power) and the FTP-anchored constraint are asserted with thin physiological justification; parameter identifiability across the 5 rider-defining parameters is not demonstrated
Cornering is collapsed to a binary 'sharp turn = speed checkpoint' with no turn radius — the authors themselves flag that ignoring corner radius weakens realism and affects low-power ride time
Recovery factor beta is held constant though they admit it should vary as energy depletes — a self-acknowledged simplification

The DP relies on discretizing a 3D state (aerobic, anaerobic, speed) x position; the paper never reports grid resolution, state-space size, or runtime, so accuracy/feasibility of the discretization is unverifiable

Some data appears thin or internally inconsistent (altitude line charts with implausible swings; the men-vs-women finish-time bar chart in Fig 12 has confusing/overlapping categories), and only a single wind data row per course is used
The team time-trial extension is largely described rather than solved — 'the solution process is not described here due to space', so Problem 5 is answered conceptually rather than with results
Wind handling only counts the head/tail-wind component (cos delta) and explicitly drops crosswind effects on stability/yaw drag
reusable_patterns: Two-layer decomposition for any 'optimal effort/resource pacing over a course' problem: a depletion/recovery budget model (energy, battery, fuel, attention) + a physics/cost model of the environment, joined by a min-time (or min-cost) DP — directly transplantable to EV range optimization, marathon pacing, drone mission energy, etc.

Discretize-the-state-space DP recipe for continuous optimal control under a hard resource constraint: state = (remaining-resource bins, terminal-velocity bins), action = effort level per segment, transition = physics + depletion; minimize cumulative time/cost

Encode a categorical taxonomy as a parameter vector and visualize on a radar chart, then feed those parameter sets back into the same model to generate per-category behavior — a clean way to satisfy 'handle different types' sub-questions

Pair two sensitivity styles in one section: a structured factorial grid sweep (here 8 dirs x 3 speeds) for exogenous conditions plus a Monte-Carlo perturbation for execution error, each reported with an explicit percentage-change threshold

Convert an abstract continuous optimum into an execution-tolerant deliverable via per-checkpoint min/max arrival-time windows that bound worst-case degradation (here +1 min) — turns a fragile optimal plan into a robust, human-followable one

Build the harder variant (team) as a minimal delta on the base model — add one coefficient (drafting drag C_dr) and one discrete decision variable (h(x) for when to swap) — to demonstrate generalizability without re-deriving everything

Open with a flowchart that explicitly wires every sub-model into the solver, and close with a standalone real-world 'briefing memo' deliverable; ground all empirical constants in cited literature and validate curves against real public data (Strava/NOAA)
